location.origin: Born April 29, 1973, in Inglewood, California. One of nine children. Parents: Eddie Aaron Atkins Jr. (1948-2013, pastor from Merced, California) and Thomasina Andrea 'Honey' Atkins (nee Daniels, evangelist and choir director from Stamford, Connecticut). Raised in the Evangelistic Church of God in Christ in Inglewood. Began singing around age twelve in church and with The Young Anointed. Studied singing formally. Appeared in Sister Act 2: Back in the Habit (1993). Backup singer for Brandy. Toured with Michael Matthews' gospel shows (Mama I'm Sorry, Sneaky) starting 1995.
location.formative: Inglewood, California: historically Black community in southwestern Los Angeles County, adjacent to Watts. Church of God in Christ (COGIC) denomination -- largest Pentecostal denomination in the US, founded 1897 in Memphis. Musical environment: full bands, Hammond organ, call-and-response worship, congregational singing. Family of nine children, all immersed in church life. Mother was choir director, father was youth minister. Met producer Warryn Campbell through gospel circuit and backup singing work. EMI publishing deal secured through Warryn Campbell. Los Angeles gospel music scene: COGIC network, traveling gospel shows, backup singing for secular R&B acts (Brandy).
location.career: Los Angeles, California (career base, 1993-present). Mary Mary formed 1998 with sister Trecina 'Tina' Atkins-Campbell. Signed to Columbia Records -- first gospel act since Tramaine Hawkins in mid-1990s. Mary Mary discography: Thankful (2000, Columbia -- #1 Gospel, #59 Top 200, Grammy), Incredible (2002, Columbia -- #1 Gospel), Mary Mary (2005, Columbia -- #8 Top 200, White House performance), A Mary Mary Christmas (2006, Columbia), The Sound (2008, Columbia -- #7 Top 200, #2 R&B, #1 Gospel 6+ months), Something Big (2011, Columbia), Go Get It (2012, Columbia -- compilation, Grammy). Hit singles: 'Shackles (Praise You)' (2000 -- #28 Hot 100, #5 R&B, #5 UK, first gospel in 5 years to crack R&B top 10, $600K video, MTV rotation, platinum), 'Heaven' (#1 Gospel Radio 9 weeks), 'Yesterday' (#10 Adult R&B), 'God in Me' ft. Kierra Sheard (2009 -- #5 R&B, #68 Hot 100, #1 Hot Dance Club Play, 64 weeks on R&B chart -- broke Dru Hill's group longevity record), 'Go Get It' (Grammy: Best Gospel Song 2013). Solo: Help (2014, Entertainment One -- #1 Gospel, #6 Billboard 200, 23K first week -- highest first-week position for female solo gospel artist, Grammy: Best Gospel Album 2015), Help 2.0 (2015, eOne -- includes 'I Luh God' ft. Big Shizz, #1 Gospel, 11M+ YouTube views, gospel trap), I Love You (2023, My Block Inc). Radio: Get Up! Mornings with Erica Campbell (May 2016-present, Reach Media/Urban One syndication, 12M weekly listeners, replaced Yolanda Adams Morning Show). Television: Mary Mary reality series on WE tv (6 seasons, March 2012-November 2017), Sunday Best judge (BET, 2007+), Kingdom Business (BET+, Season 2), Super Bowl LVI performance (2022, 'Lift Every Voice and Sing' with Youth Orchestra of LA). Ministry: California Worship Center (co-founded 2015 with husband Pastor Warryn Campbell, San Fernando CA, ~700 members). Grammy Awards: 6 wins from 18 nominations (2001 Best Contemporary Soul Gospel Album Thankful, 2009 Best Gospel Performance 'Get Up', 2010 Best Gospel Song 'God in Me', 2013 Best Gospel Song 'Go Get It', 2015 Best Gospel Album Help, 2025 Best Gospel Performance/Song 'One Hallelujah'). Other awards: 2 American Music Awards (2005, 2009), 3 NAACP Image Awards, 3 BET Awards, 1 Soul Train Award, multiple Stellar and Dove Awards. Alpha Kappa Alpha honorary member (January 15, 2023). Married Warryn Campbell May 26, 2001. Three children: Krista Nicole, Warryn III, Zaya Monique. Resides Los Angeles. Tacoma connection through gospel touring circuit and COGIC congregational network in Seattle-Tacoma metro area.

labels: Columbia Records (Mary Mary: Thankful 2000, Incredible 2002, Mary Mary 2005, A Mary Mary Christmas 2006, The Sound 2008, Something Big 2011, Go Get It 2012)
My Block Inc (solo: I Love You 2023 -- Warryn Campbell's independent label)

members.name: Erica Campbell
members.note: Born Erica Monique Atkins, April 29, 1973, Inglewood, California. One of nine siblings. COGIC-raised. Formally trained vocalist. Appeared in Sister Act 2 (1993). Backup singer for Brandy. Mary Mary co-founder (1998). Six Grammy Awards (2001, 2009, 2010, 2013, 2015, 2025). Solo debut Help #6 Billboard 200 -- highest first-week for female solo gospel artist. Host Get Up! Mornings (12M weekly listeners). First Lady of California Worship Center. Alpha Kappa Alpha honorary member. Married Warryn Campbell (2001). Three children: Krista Nicole, Warryn III, Zaya Monique.

members.name: Tina Campbell
members.note: Born Trecina Evette Atkins, May 1, 1975, Inglewood, California. Erica's younger sister. Mary Mary co-founder (1998). Solo albums: It's Personal (2015), It's Still Personal (2017 deluxe). Married musician Teddy Campbell (2000). Four children: Laiah Simone, Meela Jane, Glendon Theodore II, Santana.

associated_musicians.name: Warryn Campbell
associated_musicians.note: Born August 21, 1975, South Central Los Angeles. Erica's husband (married 2001) and primary producer for Mary Mary and solo work. Five Grammy Awards, 15 nominations spanning gospel, R&B, and rap. Produced Kanye West (Late Registration -- Grammy Best Rap Album 2006; Graduation -- Grammy Best Rap Album 2008), Alicia Keys, Yolanda Adams (Mountain High -- Grammy 2000), Missy Elliott, Brandy, Xzibit, Mos Def, DJ Quik. Started as session musician for DJ Quik and on Death Row Records projects (Murder Was the Case, Tupac). Founded My Block Inc label. VP A&R at Elektra Records. Ordained pastor 2002. Co-founded California Worship Center with Erica (2015, San Fernando CA, ~700 members).

associated_musicians.name: Kierra Sheard-Kelly
associated_musicians.note: Gospel artist. Featured on Mary Mary's 'God in Me' (2009) which reached #5 R&B and broke R&B chart longevity record for a group. Part of the Clark Sisters gospel dynasty (daughter of Karen Clark Sheard).

associated_musicians.name: Lecrae
associated_musicians.note: Christian hip-hop artist. Featured on the title track of Erica's solo debut Help. His presence on the track bridged gospel and Christian hip-hop audiences.

associated_musicians.name: Brandy
associated_musicians.note: R&B/pop star. The Atkins sisters worked as backup singers for Brandy before forming Mary Mary, learning mainstream R&B production and performance mechanics.

associated_musicians.name: Big Shizz
associated_musicians.note: Rapper featured on the gospel trap single that generated controversy and 11+ million YouTube views. The secular rap feature on a gospel track exemplified Campbell's boundary-crossing approach.

associated_musicians.name: DJ Quik
associated_musicians.note: Compton hip-hop producer who mentored Warryn Campbell. Campbell played session keyboards on DJ Quik's Safe + Sound (1995). The lineage: DJ Quik taught Warryn Campbell, who produced Mary Mary -- hip-hop production knowledge flowing into gospel.

associated_musicians.name: Tasha Cobbs Leonard
associated_musicians.note: Gospel artist. 'One Hallelujah' (with Campbell, Israel Houghton, Jonathan McReynolds, Jekalyn Carr) won Grammy for Best Gospel Performance/Song (2025) -- Campbell's most recent Grammy.

associated_musicians.name: Andrae Crouch
associated_musicians.note: Legendary gospel artist and producer. Collaborated with Mary Mary. Crouch (1942-2015) was one of the most influential figures in modern gospel music, bridging traditional and contemporary gospel.

theory_nodes.concept: The altar call (forty-third acoustic element -- the vocal technique in which a singer shifts from performance to invitation, from proclamation to summoning, calling the listener forward into participation rather than observation; the moment when the song stops being a song and becomes a directive; gospel's defining acoustic technology that demands congregational response)
examples: Erica Campbell (degree 42) -- live performances of 'Help' and 'A Little More Jesus' shift in their final minutes from the recorded arrangement into an extended vocal section that is no longer performance but invitation. The voice reaches directly toward the listener, asking for a response.

Contrast with the whiskey falsetto (degree 41, Shawn Smith) -- intimate, interior, self-directed. The altar call is communal, exterior, other-directed. Consecutive degrees, opposite vocal orientations.
Contrast with the shelved frequency (degree 40, Alice Smith) -- the suppressed voice. The altar call is the summoning voice. One silenced, the other compelling. Two degrees apart.

Connection to the congregation (degree 26, acoustic element #27) -- the engine previously mapped the congregation as listeners. The altar call maps the voice that summons them. Call and response: the call side from the gospel source.
theory_nodes.description: The altar call is the forty-third acoustic element in the Seattle 360 engine. It describes the vocal moment in gospel music where the singer shifts from performance mode into invitation mode -- from structured song into free exhortation, calling the listener to participate, to come forward, to respond. In traditional church practice, the altar call is the culmination of the worship service. In Erica Campbell's recorded and live work, it manifests as extended vocal sections that break from the arrangement into a direct, participatory address. The altar call is the fundamental distinction between gospel vocal technique and all other vocal traditions in the engine: gospel demands participation. The singer is not performing for the audience; the singer is calling the audience into the performance.

ancestors: The whiskey falsetto (degree 41, Shawn Smith) -- intimate, self-directed. The altar call is communal, summoning. Opposite orientations.
The shelved frequency (degree 40, Alice Smith) -- the suppressed voice. The altar call is the voice that will not be suppressed.
The congregation (degree 26, acoustic element #27) -- the response side. The altar call is the call side.
genealogy.relationship: Forty-three acoustic elements across forty-three degrees. One per degree, no exceptions. The altar call is the engine's first element rooted in sacred musical practice. It connects to the call-and-response tradition that originated in West Africa, traveled through the Middle Passage, and became the foundation of Black church worship. Track element 44 at degree 43.

theory_nodes.concept: The circuit rider (forty-third artist-city pattern -- an artist whose connection to a city is not through residency or birth but through the gospel touring circuit that brings them repeatedly into the city's musical and spiritual ecosystem; named for the itinerant Methodist preachers who served circuits of churches in rural America; the engine's first pattern defined by religious touring infrastructure)
examples: Erica Campbell (degree 42) -- Los Angeles-based artist whose connection to Tacoma operates through the gospel touring circuit: church conferences, gospel festivals, congregational bookings, COGIC denominational network. Not residency but periodic return through institutional channels.

Contrast with the scene native (degree 41, Shawn Smith) -- geographic presence without genre alignment. The circuit rider is periodic geographic presence through genre-specific institutional networks.

Contrast with the cultural satellite (degree 40, Alice Smith) -- genre connection without geography. The circuit rider has both genre connection AND geography, but the geography is episodic rather than continuous.
Historical reference: Methodist circuit riders of the 18th-19th centuries who traveled circuits of churches on horseback, belonging to no single community but serving all of them.
theory_nodes.description: The circuit rider is the forty-third artist-city pattern in the Seattle 360 engine. It describes an artist whose connection to a city is not through residency, birth, or recording, but through the touring circuit -- specifically the gospel touring circuit -- that brings them into the city's ecosystem with regularity. The term comes from American religious history: itinerant preachers who traveled circuits of churches in rural America. Erica Campbell's relationship to Tacoma and the Pacific Northwest operates through the gospel touring circuit: COGIC congregational networks, church conferences, gospel music festivals, and concert bookings. Gospel music has a different geography than secular music -- its cities are connected by church networks, not by record labels or radio markets. The circuit rider is the engine's first pattern defined by religious touring infrastructure.

ancestors: The scene native (degree 41, Shawn Smith) -- geographic presence without genre alignment. The circuit rider is periodic presence through genre-specific networks.
The cultural satellite (degree 40, Alice Smith) -- genre without geography. The circuit rider has both, but episodically.
The touring anchor (degree 38, Booker T. Jones) -- touring as connection mechanism. The circuit rider is touring through religious rather than secular infrastructure.
genealogy.relationship: Forty-three artist-city patterns across forty-three degrees. The circuit rider is the first pattern defined by religious touring infrastructure. It reveals gospel's invisible geography: cities connected by congregational networks rather than record label distribution. Track pattern 44 at degree 43.

theory_nodes.concept: Blue note -- the sanctified bend (forty-second deployment; the blue note that bends upward rather than downward, reaching toward transcendence rather than dwelling in lament; gospel's theological proposition made acoustic: suffering is real but not final; the note that resolves from petition into praise)
examples: Erica Campbell (degree 42) -- vocal performances that enact the duality of acknowledgment and transcendence. The verse sits in the struggle (blue note's downward pull); the chorus lifts into affirmation (upward bend). The blue note is not eliminated in gospel -- it is transformed.

Contrast with the interior blue (degree 41, Shawn Smith) -- inward-directed, self-contained. The sanctified bend is outward-directed, upward-reaching. Consecutive blue notes with opposite trajectories.
Contrast with the suppressed vibration (degree 40, Alice Smith) -- institutional displacement. The sanctified bend is spiritual uplift.

Blues and gospel share a root system: both process suffering through sound. Blues processes suffering into expression. Gospel processes suffering into deliverance. The blue note is the common ground; the direction of the bend is the difference.
theory_nodes.description: The sanctified bend is the forty-second blue note deployment in the Seattle 360 engine. It describes the blue note that bends upward rather than downward -- from tension into release, from petition into praise, from the cry to the response. The conventional blue note creates tension by pulling a melody toward sadness, ambiguity, or unresolved emotion. The sanctified bend resolves upward. This is the acoustic signature of gospel music's theological proposition: suffering is real but not final. Every gospel performance contains both the acknowledgment of pain and the insistence on transcendence. The blue note does not disappear in gospel; it is transformed. The sanctified bend is the sound of that transformation.

ancestors: Blue note -- the interior blue (degree 41, Shawn Smith): inward trajectory. The sanctified bend is upward trajectory.
Blue note -- the suppressed vibration (degree 40, Alice Smith): institutional constraint. The sanctified bend is spiritual release.
All forty-one prior blue note deployments (degrees 0-41)
genealogy.relationship: Forty-two deployments across forty-three degrees. The sanctified bend follows the interior blue (41) -- inward containment followed by upward release. Gospel's blue note does not eliminate the blues tradition's blue note; it redirects it. Track deployment 43 at degree 43.

theory_nodes.concept: E=5 return mode (ninth E=5 deployment -- energy returns to E=5 after one degree of E=4 recalibration at degree 41; the nine-degree E=5+ streak (32-40) paused but did not collapse; distribution 2:13:7:12:9:1; E=5 now tied with E=4 frequency; gospel's commercial and cultural breakthrough energy)
examples: Erica Campbell (degree 42) -- E=5 return. 'Shackles (Praise You)' #28 Hot 100, #5 R&B. The Sound #7 Billboard 200. 'God in Me' 64-week R&B chart run (broke group longevity record). Help #6 Billboard 200 (highest for female solo gospel artist). 6 Grammy Awards. 12 million weekly radio listeners. E=5 measures the trajectory of an artist who brought gospel into every room.

E=5 RETURNS after E=4 interior mode at degree 41 (Shawn Smith). One degree of recalibration, then the high-energy zone resumes. The nine-degree streak (32-40) was not an anomaly but a structural feature of this engine sector.
Distribution after degree 42: 2:13:7:12:9:1 (E=1:E=2:E=3:E=4:E=5:E=6). E=5 count rises from 8 to 9. E=5 now tied with E=4 as second-most-frequent energy level.
Contrast with E=4 interior mode (degree 41, Shawn Smith) -- immense talent filtered through obscurity. E=5 return is immense talent amplified through commercial and cultural channels.
theory_nodes.description: E=5 return mode is the ninth E=5 deployment in the Seattle 360 engine. It registers the return to E=5 after one degree of E=4 recalibration (degree 41, Shawn Smith). The nine-consecutive-E=5+ streak (32-40) paused at degree 41 and resumes at degree 42 -- the streak did not collapse, it breathed. E=5 for Erica Campbell measures the trajectory of an artist whose commercial and cultural impact fundamentally redefined the commercial possibilities of gospel music. Six Grammy Awards, Billboard 200 top-10 albums, R&B chart records, a nationally syndicated radio show, a Super Bowl performance -- the energy is at scale.

ancestors: E=4 interior mode (degree 41, Shawn Smith, E=4) -- recalibration. E=5 return is the resumption after the pause.
Label-defiant tidal (degree 40, Alice Smith, E=5) -- energy from institutional resistance. Return mode is energy from institutional mastery.
Breakthrough tidal (degree 35, Robert Cray, E=5) -- energy from escape velocity. Return mode confirms the sustained high-energy sector.
genealogy.relationship: Nine E=5 modes across forty-three degrees. Distribution: 2:13:7:12:9:1. E=5 ties E=4 in frequency. The E=5 return after E=4 at degree 41 confirms the blues/soul/R&B/gospel quadrant as a sustained high-energy zone. Track at degree 43.

theory_nodes.concept: Gospel/R&B genre stage (Genre Stage 23 -- the first gospel genre stage in the engine; gospel is the upstream source of soul, R&B, funk, and blues; at degree 42, the engine maps the headwater; the R&B qualifier acknowledges the fusion production that brought gospel into mainstream commercial space)
examples: Erica Campbell (degree 42) -- Gospel/R&B. Gospel primary (COGIC-raised, church ministry, sacred lyrical content, altar call vocal technique). R&B secondary (production style: hip-hop beats, R&B melodic structure, trap elements in 'I Luh God', Warryn Campbell's crossover production).

Ray Charles (degree 30) -- secularized gospel into soul. Campbell brought R&B production into gospel. Same crossing, opposite directions. Charles went downstream; Campbell stays at the source and redirects the tributary.
Genre Stage progression: Soul/Blues (41) -> Gospel/R&B (42). The engine has been documenting downstream genres for 41 degrees. At degree 42, it documents the upstream source.

The R&B qualifier is essential: Mary Mary's innovation was gospel message in R&B packaging. 'Shackles' charted higher on R&B than gospel. 'God in Me' held an R&B longevity record. The genre compound acknowledges the fusion.
theory_nodes.description: Gospel/R&B at degree 42 is Genre Stage 23 in the Seattle 360 engine -- the first time gospel appears as a primary genre element. Gospel is the upstream source of virtually every genre the engine has documented: soul emerged from gospel, R&B from soul, funk from R&B, blues shares roots with gospel in the African American spiritual tradition. For forty-one degrees, the engine documented the children. At degree 42, it documents the parent. The R&B qualifier acknowledges that Erica Campbell's music is not traditional gospel but a hybrid form that brought gospel into R&B's commercial space -- gospel message delivered through R&B production.

ancestors: Soul/Blues (degree 41, Shawn Smith) -- Genre Stage 22. Secular soul. Gospel/R&B is the sacred counterpart.
Neo-Soul (degree 40, Alice Smith) -- Genre Stage 21. Neo-Soul derived from soul which derived from gospel.
Soul/Gospel (degree 29, Ivan Boogaloo Joe Jones) -- soul compound with gospel qualifier. Degree 42 reverses the compound: gospel primary, secular qualifier.
genealogy.relationship: Twenty-three genre stages across forty-three degrees. Gospel/R&B is the first gospel-primary stage. The genre map now includes the source tradition from which all prior stages descended. Track at degree 43.

seattle_connection: CIRCUIT RIDER -- Erica Campbell (degree 42) connects to Tacoma through the gospel touring circuit rather than residency. Born and based in Los Angeles. The COGIC denominational network, church conferences, gospel festivals, and congregational bookings bring gospel artists into Pacific Northwest cities with regularity. The Total Experience Gospel Choir (1973, Pat Wright) drew members from Seattle, Everett, Auburn, and Tacoma. Quincy Jones (degree 0) sang in a gospel group in Bremerton. Gospel's roots in the region predate the jazz tradition the engine has documented. FIRST GOSPEL ARTIST in the engine -- genre expansion landmark. Gospel is the upstream source of soul, R&B, funk, and blues documented in degrees 0-41. THE ALTAR CALL as 43rd acoustic element -- the voice that shifts from performance to invitation, gospel's defining vocal technology. THE CIRCUIT RIDER as 43rd artist-city pattern -- connection through religious touring infrastructure rather than residency. THE SANCTIFIED BEND as 42nd blue note -- the note that bends upward into transcendence. E=5 RETURNS after one degree of E=4 recalibration (degree 41). Distribution 2:13:7:12:9:1. GOSPEL/R&B at Genre Stage 23 -- first gospel genre stage. TACOMA RETURNS as third Tacoma degree (after 33, 34). EIGHTH WOMAN in the engine (~18.6%). Six Grammy Awards from 18 nominations (2001-2025). 'Shackles (Praise You)' #28 Hot 100, #5 R&B. 'God in Me' 64 weeks R&B (group longevity record). Help #6 Billboard 200 (female solo gospel record). Get Up! Mornings radio: 12M weekly listeners. California Worship Center: ~700 members. Mary Mary: 6 studio albums, 6 WE tv seasons, Super Bowl LVI. Husband Warryn Campbell: producer for Kanye West, Alicia Keys, 5 Grammys. COGIC-raised, Columbia-signed, Grammy-winning, radio-hosting, church-pastoring. The headwater.

theory_genealogy: The altar call (NEW at degree 42): 43rd acoustic element. Vocal shift from performance to invitation. Gospel's defining vocal technology. Track element 44 at degree 43.

The circuit rider (NEW at degree 42): 43rd artist-city pattern. Connection through gospel touring circuit rather than residency. First pattern defined by religious infrastructure. Track pattern 44 at degree 43.
Blue note -- the sanctified bend (NEW at degree 42): 42nd deployment. Upward-bending blue note. Gospel's theological proposition made acoustic. Track deployment 43 at degree 43.
E=5 return mode (NEW at degree 42): 9th E=5 deployment. Returns after E=4 recalibration at 41. Streak paused not collapsed. Track at degree 43.
Gospel/R&B genre stage (NEW at degree 42): Genre Stage 23. First gospel genre stage. The source enters the engine. Track at degree 43.
engine_sequencing: E=5 RETURNS at 42: Distribution 2:13:7:12:9:1. Return mode as 9th E=5. One degree of E=4 recalibration (41) then E=5 resumes. E=5 now tied with E=4 in frequency. Track at degree 43.
GENRE Stage 23 at degree 42: Gospel/R&B. First gospel genre stage. Source tradition enters the record. Track at degree 43.
FIRST GOSPEL ARTIST at degree 42: Genre expansion landmark. All prior genres descended from gospel. Track gospel thread.
TACOMA returns at degree 42: Third Tacoma degree (after 33, 34). Circuit rider connection vs residency (33) and group membership (34). Track Tacoma thread.
ALTAR CALL as 43rd acoustic element: First sacred-music-rooted element. Call side of call-and-response (congregation was response side at degree 26). Track element 44 at degree 43.
CIRCUIT RIDER as 43rd artist-city pattern: First pattern defined by religious touring infrastructure. Track at degree 43.
SANCTIFIED BEND as 42nd blue note: First upward-bending blue note. Gospel transforms the blue note. Track deployment 43 at degree 43.
COLUMBIA RECORDS at degree 42: Same label that signed Mary Mary also had Alice Smith (40) and Brad/Shawn Smith (41). Three consecutive Columbia/Sony degrees (40-42). Track label thread.
EIGHTH WOMAN at degree 42: Gender ratio 8:35 (~18.6%). Two women in three degrees (40, 42) is densest female representation in engine history. Track.
All engine sequencing items from degrees 0-41
cultural_patterns: Artist-city taxonomy: FORTY-THREE named patterns (adding The Circuit Rider at degree 42). First religious-infrastructure pattern. Track.
Gender ratio: 8:35 (women:men). ~18.6%. Erica Campbell = female. Two women in three degrees (40, 42). Track.
GOSPEL enters the engine: The source tradition. All prior genres descended from gospel. Sacred music mapped for the first time. Track gospel thread.
TACOMA returns: Third degree (33, 34, 42). Three different connection modes: residency (33), group membership (34), circuit rider (42). Track.
E=5 RETURNS: One degree of E=4 recalibration, then E=5 resumes. The sustained high-energy zone (32-40) was structural, not anomalous. Track.

WARRYN CAMPBELL as cross-genre bridge: Same producer for Mary Mary (gospel), Kanye West (hip-hop), Alicia Keys (R&B). Five Grammys spanning three genres. Gospel-hip-hop-R&B are one musical language in his practice.
COLUMBIA/SONY at three consecutive degrees (40-42): Alice Smith (shelved), Shawn Smith/Brad (released), Mary Mary (breakthrough). Same parent company, three different outcomes. Track.
COGIC denominational network: First explicit denominational connection in the engine. Track whether future degrees map other denominational networks.
CHURCH FOUNDING: California Worship Center. Artist-to-pastor trajectory. The stage and the pulpit converge. Track.
All cultural pattern items from degrees 0-41
